participants.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official eligibility criteria, recruitment setting, exclusion criteria, and overall participant characteristics closely, with only minor differences due to reporting subgroup versus total sample details.
participants.extracted: The study included sick-listed employed adults aged 18 years and older who had been on sickness absence for 4 to 26 weeks and screened positive on at least one mental health screening measure: depression (PHQ-9 score ≥10), somatization (PHQ-15 score ≥10), or anxiety (GAD-7 score ≥10). Participants were recruited from employees of small- to medium-sized companies receiving occupational health services in the Netherlands, with some employees also recruited through a large mental health service employer. Exclusion criteria were insufficient Dutch language skills, pregnancy, involvement in legal action against the employer, or no Internet access. In total, 220 participants were enrolled, with a mean age of 44.2 years; 59% were women, about 70% were married or living together, and most had Dutch nationality.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted intervention matches the official description in core components, including the ECO blended program, half-day physician training, 16-module web-based CBT/PST content, and care-as-usual control.
interventions.extracted: The intervention was ECO (E-health module embedded in Collaborative Occupational health care), a blended return-to-work program for sick-listed employees with common mental disorders. It combined two components: an employee-facing web-based module, Return@Work, and an automated email decision aid for the occupational physician. Return@Work provided individualized, tailored sessions covering psychoeducation, cognitions about returning to work while still symptomatic based on CBT principles, problem-solving treatment exercises, pain/fatigue management and reactivation, and relapse prevention; in total the module included 16 sessions, with participants receiving a tailored subset ranging from 6 to 17 sessions depending on symptoms and return-to-work cognitions. The module also regularly monitored functioning and mental/physical symptoms. Occupational physicians in the intervention arm were trained for half a day and received automated email advice based on the monitoring data to support stepped-care treatment, symptom and function monitoring, referral to appropriate care in the curative sector when progress was insufficient, and access to a consultant psychiatrist for advice if stagnation occurred. In the control condition, occupational physicians provided care as usual according to Dutch occupational medicine guidelines, without the ECO module or decision-aid support.
interventions.official:
Intervention characteristics
Work‐directed intervention combined with clinical intervention
Content: The intervention was made up of 1. a decision aid including half a day training for occupational health physicians 2. a 16 module web‐based CBT/PST intervention for sick listed employees
Duration, frequency, length: Half a day training for physicians; it is unclear if there was a time limit for the participants in using the web‐modules
Communication means: Web‐based for the CBT and problem solvin

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include multiple return-to-work and mental health measures, whereas the official outcome only reports sick leave days at end of follow-up as a continuous sickness absence measure.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure a primary outcome of time until first return to work (RTW), defined as the number of calendar days from randomization until the first partial or full RTW, and a secondary primary-related outcome of time until full RTW (days from randomization until lasting full RTW), both assessed using occupational health service or employer register data over 12 months. It also planned to track the total number of sickness absence days during the first year of follow-up from register data. Secondary outcomes were common mental disorder symptoms measured by self-report questionnaires at baseline and 3, 6, 9, and 12 months: depression severity with the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), anxiety severity with the Generalized Anxiety Disorder 7-item scale (GAD-7), and somatization severity with the Patient Health Questionnaire-15 (PHQ-15). For these symptom scales, response was defined as a 50% reduction from baseline and remission as a score below 5, evaluated at each follow-up time point among participants with baseline scores above the cutoff. Additional planned covariates included demographics, chronic medical illness, job characteristics, and intention to return to work despite symptoms. Process measures included health care utilization with the Trimbos/iMTA questionnaire for Costs associated with Psychiatric illness (TiC-P) at 3 months and intervention adherence metrics (log-ins, module initiation/completion, and psychiatrist consultation) in the ECO group.
